hypothesis: The `gated` family may only be competitive at the largest support, so stepping `K` down from `128` to `96` while holding `EF=12`, `optimizer=signum`, and `lr=1.6e-3` tests one question only: does `gated` remain competitive on the adjacent frontier point, or is its apparent edge confined to `K=128`?
summary: Round 35 answered the preprocess probe: Hadamard did not improve the anchored `gated` recipe, leaving no meaningful recipe knobs unexplored in the prescribed order. The next single-variable move is therefore `K`. Use the plain `gated` anchor and drop only `K` to `96`. This is the highest-value neighboring check because there is already a strong `topk` control at `K=96` (`0.171856`), so the run directly tells us whether `gated` has broader family viability or whether it should be treated as a narrow `K=128` specialization. Read the curve shape as well as the endpoint: if `gated@96` is smooth and beats or matches the `topk@96` control comfortably, keep the family alive across K; if it is clearly worse or unstable, treat `gated` as exhausted outside the single `K=128` point.
self_review: This keeps the same family, preserves EF at 12, changes exactly one primary variable (`K`), and only moves K after the LR, optimizer, loss, and preprocess probes failed to improve the anchored recipe. Choosing `K=96` minimizes extrapolation because it is adjacent to the current gated anchor and has a matched topk frontier point for direct comparison.
notes_to_memory: Round 36 begins the first K probe for gated after recipe tuning was exhausted: gated, K=96, EF=12, signum, LR=1.6e-3.
Compare gated@96 against the existing topk@96 control and judge curve shape, not just terminal FVU; this determines whether gated generalizes beyond the isolated K=128 win.
next_hypotheses: If `gated@96` is competitive with or better than `topk@96`, gated deserves at least one more K-mapping round within the allowed grid.
If `gated@96` clearly trails `topk@96`, stop spending mainline rounds on gated except as the recorded K=128 incumbent.
